suggested.title: [For Student] The Odyssey
suggested.author: Homer (Robert Fagles or Richmond Lattimore translation)

suggested.title: [For Student] The Way of the Warrior
suggested.author: or equivalent introduction to Bushido, Lakota warrior codes, or other Indigenous martial ethics

supplementary.title: [For Student] Brief selections from the Bible: Proverbs, the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5-7)
[For Mentor] A Short History of Ethics
supplementary.author: Alasdair MacIntyre

competence_markers.description: The student can describe at least four distinct virtue systems (Greek, Stoic, Christian, Confucian, Indigenous/warrior) and accurately identify their core emphases
The student has engaged with primary texts from at least three traditions and can quote or paraphrase key passages
The student can write a comparative analysis of two traditions' treatment of the same virtue, steelmanning both
The student can identify virtue failures in historical figures and explain which virtue failed and why
The student can articulate the significance of cross-cultural convergence on core virtues
The student demonstrates genuine engagement with the material — asking questions, expressing honest disagreement, making connections across traditions — rather than performing expected answers
The student can steelman a tradition they find personally unconvincing, presenting its strongest case fairly
Case study discussions show increasing sophistication — the student identifies competing goods, references multiple traditions, and acknowledges complexity rather than reaching for simple answers

narration_prompts.prompt: Explain Aristotle's claim that virtue is a habit, not a feeling.
narration_prompts.detail: What does he mean? Do you agree? How does your experience in IC1.1 confirm or challenge this idea?

narration_prompts.prompt: Compare the Greek and Confucian approaches to virtue.
narration_prompts.detail: What does Aristotle emphasize that Confucius does not? What does Confucius emphasize that Aristotle does not? Which approach resonates more with you, and why?

narration_prompts.prompt: What do the Stoics mean when they say "some things are within our power and some are not"?
narration_prompts.detail: How does this principle change how a person practices virtue? Give a concrete example from your own life.

narration_prompts.prompt: How does the Christian tradition's addition of love, faith, and hope change the classical virtue system?
narration_prompts.detail: What can a Christian virtue ethic demand that a purely Greek one cannot? Is this an improvement?

narration_prompts.prompt: Choose a historical figure who failed morally despite knowing what virtue required.
narration_prompts.detail: What went wrong? Which virtue system would have best addressed their failure, and why?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the significance of the convergence?
narration_prompts.detail: Why does it matter that civilizations which never met each other arrived at similar conclusions about virtue? What does this convergence tell us about moral truth?

cross_connections.description: The self-directed reading and writing practices in IC1.2-B depend on the planning infrastructure developed in IC1.1-B1. The student who cannot manage their study time will not complete the primary text readings.
The virtue of honesty, studied comparatively here, is lived practically in IC1.3. The historical failures analyzed in IC1.2-B provide case material for IC1.3's exploration of accountability.

The virtue systems studied in IC1.2-B belong to the civilizations studied in Domain 1. The Greek cardinal virtues are inseparable from Athenian democracy and its collapse. Roman Stoicism is inseparable from the Republic's fall and the Empire's rise. The historical context enriches the moral analysis.

The virtue traditions in IC1.2-B are grounded in the larger worldview systems studied in 1.3. The Christian virtues make sense within a Christian metaphysics. The Confucian virtues make sense within a Confucian social order. Studying the worldview context deepens the virtue analysis.

The ability to steelman different traditions, developed in IC1.2-B, is directly applicable to the critical thinking skills required by 1.7. A person who can understand multiple moral frameworks on their own terms is far harder to capture ideologically.
The comparative analyses and steelman exercises in IC1.2-B develop the same structured argumentation skills built in the writing track.

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you met virtue through stories. You learned the names. You recognized courage, honesty, justice, and the rest in the heroes and anti-heroes of many traditions. You began to develop moral intuition — a felt sense of right and wrong built through hundreds of encounters with characters making difficult choices.

Now you go deeper.

Weathering Storms moves from stories to systems. Every major civilization did not merely tell stories about courage and justice — they built entire frameworks for thinking about what it means to be a good person. The Greeks developed one system. The Romans adapted it and added their own. The Chinese developed a completely different one. The Christian tradition built on the Greek while adding something the Greeks never imagined. Indigenous warrior cultures codified honor in ways that were practiced, not theorized.

You are going to study these systems directly. Not summaries about what Aristotle said — Aristotle's actual words. Not a textbook description of Stoicism — Marcus Aurelius writing in his own journal at the end of a long day of governing an empire. Not someone explaining Confucius — Confucius himself, teaching his students.

This matters because moral reasoning from principle requires understanding how principles were developed. A person who says "courage is good" without understanding why the Greeks, Romans, Chinese, Christians, and Indigenous warriors all concluded this — independently — has an opinion. A person who has studied the convergence and understands the reasoning has something far more powerful: a foundation for moral judgment that cannot be swept away by fashion, pressure, or rhetoric.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Comparative moral analysis** — the ability to examine virtue systems from different civilizations side by side, identifying convergence and divergence
2. **Primary source engagement** — reading the actual words of moral philosophers and leaders, not just summaries about them
3. **Understanding virtue as system** — recognizing that isolated virtues exist within larger frameworks of human purpose and social order
4. **Steel-manning different traditions** — the ability to understand each system on its own terms before comparing or critiquing
5. **Historical moral reasoning** — applying virtue analysis to real historical figures and their decisions
6. **Recognition of convergence** — the discovery that independent civilizations arrived at remarkably similar conclusions about character
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Greek System — Arete and the Cardinal Virtues

The Greeks did not just tell stories about virtue. They built a philosophical system around it. The word they used was *arete* — often translated as "virtue" but more accurately meaning "excellence" or "the quality that makes something what it is supposed to be." A knife with *arete* cuts well. A horse with *arete* runs well. A human with *arete* lives well — which means lives according to the virtues that make a human life excellent.

**Aristotle's key insight: virtue is a habit, not a feeling.**

In the *Nicomachean Ethics*, Aristotle argues that you do not become courageous by thinking about courage. You become courageous by doing courageous things. You do not become honest by believing honesty is good. You become honest by telling the truth, again and again, until truthfulness becomes your default. Virtue is a *hexis* — a stable disposition acquired through practice.

This should sound familiar. It is exactly what IC1.1 has been building. Aristotle would have recognized the daily routine, the chore ownership, the practice of waiting — these are habit formation, which is virtue formation. You have been doing Aristotle's program without reading Aristotle.

**The doctrine of the mean.** Aristotle taught that every virtue is a balance between two extremes — two vices. Courage is the mean between cowardice (too little) and recklessness (too much). Generosity is the mean between stinginess and wastefulness. Honesty is the mean between deceitfulness and brutal tactlessness. The virtuous person finds the right amount, in the right situation, for the right reasons. This is why virtue requires prudence (*phronesis*) — practical wisdom. You cannot follow a formula. You must judge each situation.

**The four cardinal virtues.** The Greeks identified four virtues as foundational — virtues that every other virtue depends on:
- **Courage** (*andreia*) — acting rightly under threat
- **Temperance** (*sophrosyne*) — self-governance of appetite and desire
- **Justice** (*dikaiosyne*) — giving what is owed
- **Prudence** (*phronesis*) — practical wisdom, the ability to judge well

These four have been recognized as foundational by virtually every Western moral tradition since. They are not the only virtues — but they are the load-bearing ones.

**Reading assignment:** Read Aristotle, *Nicomachean Ethics*, Book II, Chapters 1-4. It is difficult. Read it slowly. Read it twice. Write down in your own words: what does Aristotle say virtue is? How does it develop? What is the doctrine of the mean?

### Part 2: The Roman System — Virtus and Stoic Self-Governance

The Romans inherited Greek philosophy but shaped it to their own character. Where the Greeks were theoretical, the Romans were practical. The Roman word *virtus* originally meant manliness or strength — the qualities that made a Roman citizen capable of service to the Republic. Over time, it expanded to include the full range of character strengths, but it always retained an emphasis on action, duty, and endurance.

**Stoicism: the philosophy of self-governance.**

The Stoic philosophers — Seneca, Epictetus, Marcus Aurelius — developed the most practical virtue system in Western history. Their core insight: **you cannot control what happens to you, but you can control how you respond.** Therefore, virtue consists in governing your responses — your judgments, your emotions, your actions — regardless of external circumstances.

Marcus Aurelius was the Roman Emperor — the most powerful person in the Western world. Every night, he wrote in his private journal (which we now call the *Meditations*) about his failures, his temptations, and his commitment to virtue. He was not writing for an audience. He was writing to hold himself accountable. Read his words:

> "When you wake in the morning, tell yourself: The people I deal with today will be meddling, ungrateful, arrogant, dishonest, jealous, and surly. They are like this because they cannot tell good from evil. But I have seen the beauty of good, and the ugliness of evil, and have recognized that the wrongdoer has a nature related to my own — not of the same blood or birth, but the same mind, and possessing a share of the divine. And so none of them can injure me."

This is a man with absolute power reminding himself, privately, not to be destroyed by the behavior of difficult people. That is self-governance at the highest level.

**Epictetus and the Stoic starting point.** Epictetus was a former slave. His *Enchiridion* opens with the most important Stoic principle: "Some things are within our power, and some things are not. Within our power are opinion, motivation, desire, aversion — whatever is our own doing. Not within our power are our body, our property, our reputation, our office — whatever is not our own doing."

The Stoic virtue system follows from this division. Since you cannot control external events, investing your character in external outcomes makes you fragile. Since you CAN control your judgments and your will, investing your character in how you respond makes you strong. A Stoic is not someone who feels nothing — that is a misunderstanding. A Stoic is someone whose character does not depend on things outside their control.

**Reading assignment:** Read Marcus Aurelius, *Meditations*, Book II. Then read Epictetus, *Enchiridion*, sections 1-5. In your own words: what do the Stoics say you can control? What happens to a person who builds their life around things they cannot control?

### Part 3: The Christian System — Theological Virtues and the Transformation of the Classical

Christianity inherited the four cardinal virtues from the Greeks and added three of its own — the theological virtues:
- **Faith** — trust in and commitment to God and the good, even without full understanding
- **Hope** — the orientation toward a future good, sustained through present suffering
- **Love** (*agape*, *caritas*) — self-giving care for others, including enemies, that seeks their genuine good

The addition of these three virtues represents a radical departure from the classical world. The Greeks admired strength, excellence, and wisdom. The Christians added something the Greeks would have found puzzling: the virtue of self-sacrifice for others, including those who have wronged you. "Love your enemies. Do good to those who hate you." This is not a natural human instinct. It is a demand that runs against the grain of every classical virtue system — and yet it has been one of the most influential moral ideas in human history.

**Thomas Aquinas and the synthesis.** In the 13th century, Thomas Aquinas achieved the most complete integration of Greek and Christian virtue theory. He argued that the four cardinal virtues are accessible to human reason — any thoughtful person can discover them. The three theological virtues require something more: they are gifts that orient a person toward an end beyond merely human flourishing. The result is a seven-virtue system that combines classical wisdom with Christian aspiration.

**The Christian emphasis on humility.** For the Greeks, humility was not a virtue — it was closer to a weakness. The Greek hero was proud of his excellence and expected recognition. The Christian tradition reversed this: humility — accurate self-knowledge, including knowledge of one's dependence and limitations — became central. "Blessed are the meek." This is not weakness. It is strength that does not need to display itself.

**Reading assignment:** Read the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5-7). Then read Proverbs chapters 1-3. In your own words: how does the Christian vision of virtue differ from the Greek? What does Christianity add? Where does it agree with the classical tradition?

### Part 4: The Confucian System — Virtue Through Relationship

Confucius (Kong Qiu, 551-479 BC) developed a virtue system that differs fundamentally from both the Greek and the Christian in one crucial respect: virtue is not primarily about individual character. It is about how you fulfill your roles in a web of relationships.

**The five key relationships:**
1. Ruler and subject
2. Parent and child
3. Husband and wife
4. Elder sibling and younger sibling
5. Friend and friend

Each relationship carries specific obligations. The parent protects and guides. The child honors and obeys. The ruler governs justly. The subject serves loyally. Virtue consists not in abstract excellence but in fulfilling these relational obligations with genuine care.

**Core Confucian virtues:**
- **Ren** (仁) — humaneness, benevolence, the quality of genuinely caring about others' well-being. Often translated as "goodness." Ren is the master virtue — the one that animates all the others.
- **Yi** (义) — righteousness, the sense of moral duty that leads you to do what is right even when it costs you. Yi is what makes you act even when ren alone might not.
- **Li** (礼) — ritual propriety, the discipline of behaving correctly according to your role and context. Li is not mindless conformity — it is the external expression of internal virtue. When you treat your elders with respect, you are practicing li. When that respect is genuine, li and ren are united.
- **Zhi** (智) — wisdom, the capacity to judge well.
- **Xin** (信) — trustworthiness, keeping your word.

**The Confucian insight:** You do not become virtuous in isolation. You become virtuous through your relationships — by fulfilling your obligations to the people who depend on you and to whom you owe something. A "virtuous hermit" is nearly a contradiction in Confucian terms. Virtue is social. It is demonstrated in how you treat your parents, your teachers, your community, and your subordinates.

**Reading assignment:** Read *The Analects*, Book I and Book IV. In your own words: how does Confucius define a "gentleman" (junzi)? What does virtue look like in Confucian thought that is different from how the Greeks describe it?

### Part 5: Indigenous and Warrior Traditions — Virtue as Lived Code

The virtue systems above are philosophical — developed through writing, teaching, and systematic argument. But many of the world's most powerful virtue traditions were never written down as philosophy. They were lived as codes — embedded in daily practice, rites of passage, and community expectations.

**The Lakota virtues.** The Lakota people identified four cardinal virtues: *wacantognaka* (generosity), *woohitika* (bravery), *wowacintanka* (fortitude), and *woksape* (wisdom). These were not taught in classrooms. They were taught through practice — through hunts, ceremonies, acts of service, and the stories elders told. A Lakota warrior proved generosity by giving away possessions, not accumulating them. Bravery was demonstrated in action, not discussed in theory.

**Bushido — the way of the warrior.** The Japanese samurai tradition codified eight virtues: righteousness, courage, benevolence, respect, honesty, honor, loyalty, and self-control. Bushido was not a philosophy to be debated. It was a code to be lived — and the failure to live it brought shame not only on the individual but on their family and lord. The samurai's virtue was tested every day, not in examinations, but in how they carried themselves, treated others, and met danger.

**What these traditions share with the philosophical systems:** Courage. Self-governance. Justice. Loyalty. Responsibility. The warrior traditions arrived at the same virtues as the philosophers — but through practice rather than theory, through story rather than argument, through lived code rather than written treatise.

**What they add:** A directness that philosophical systems sometimes lack. The warrior does not theorize about courage. The warrior is courageous or the warrior is disgraced. There is no comfortable distance between the idea and the act.

**Reading assignment:** Research one warrior or Indigenous virtue tradition in depth (Lakota, Bushido, Spartan *agoge*, Apache, Maori, or another). Write a one-page summary: What were the core virtues? How were they taught? How were they tested? How do they compare to the Greek cardinal virtues?

### Part 6: The Convergence — What It Means That Everyone Agrees

You have now studied five distinct traditions — Greek, Roman/Stoic, Christian, Confucian, and Indigenous/warrior. They developed independently, on different continents, across thousands of years. And yet:

Every one of them values courage.
Every one of them values honesty or trustworthiness.
Every one of them values justice or righteousness.
Every one of them values self-governance — whether they call it temperance, Stoic discipline, li, or self-control.
Every one of them values responsibility to others.
Every one of them expects that virtue will be costly.

The differences are real and instructive. The Greeks emphasize individual excellence. Confucius emphasizes relational obligation. The Christians add self-sacrificial love. The Stoics focus on internal freedom. The warriors embed virtue in code and consequence. These differences matter — they shape how each tradition handles edge cases, conflicts between virtues, and the question of what the highest virtue is.

But the convergence matters more. When civilizations that never communicated with each other arrive at the same core conclusions about what makes a person good, you are no longer looking at cultural preference. You are looking at something closer to moral reality — truths about human character that are as discoverable as truths about physics or biology.

This convergence is the central claim of IC1.2: **virtue is not arbitrary. It is discovered.** Every serious civilization discovered it. The traditions differ on emphasis, ordering, and expression. They agree on substance.

Your task in Weathering Storms is to understand each tradition well enough to steelman it — to present its best case, on its own terms — and to recognize the convergence without erasing the differences.

### Part 7: Virtue Failures in History — Where Character Broke

Understanding virtue systems is not enough. You must also study where those systems failed to prevent moral catastrophe — where people who knew the virtues, and perhaps even believed in them, failed to live them.

**Alexander the Great** studied under Aristotle himself. He learned the cardinal virtues from the philosopher who systematized them. He demonstrated extraordinary courage and tactical brilliance. And he also murdered friends in drunken rages, demanded to be worshipped as a god, and destroyed cities that resisted him. Aristotle's student — and yet temperance, justice, and humility were abandoned when power removed external constraints.

**The antebellum American South** was filled with people who attended church, read their Bibles, and spoke of Christian virtue — while enslaving human beings. The Christian virtue of love, applied selectively, became compatible with monstrous injustice. How? This question is not rhetorical. It demands serious analysis: how did a virtue system fail to prevent the evil it should have made impossible?

**The samurai class of feudal Japan** practiced Bushido — and also enforced a rigid caste system, executed peasants for minor disrespect, and maintained their honor through violence that often had nothing to do with justice. A code of virtue that applies only within your class is incomplete.

These failures do not invalidate the virtue systems. They reveal something critical: **knowing the virtues is not the same as living them.** Every tradition warns about this gap. Aristotle says virtue requires practice, not just knowledge. The Christians warn against hypocrisy. Confucius distinguishes the *junzi* (exemplary person) from the *xiaoren* (petty person) precisely on the question of whether virtue is genuine or performed.

The study of virtue failure is not cynicism. It is realism. You are being equipped not merely to admire virtue but to understand the conditions under which it fails — so that your own practice of virtue can be honest, tested, and resilient.
content.formative_practices:
### Practice 1: Primary Text Reading [Weekly]
Read one primary text selection per week from the assigned traditions. Read slowly. Read twice if needed. After reading, write a short response (half page minimum) answering: what is the author's main claim about virtue? Do you agree? What questions does this raise?

### Practice 2: Comparative Analysis [Biweekly]
Every two weeks, write a comparison of two traditions' treatment of the same virtue. For example: "How do Aristotle and Confucius each understand justice?" The comparison should steelman both traditions — presenting each at its strongest — before noting differences.

### Practice 3: Historical Virtue Analysis [Monthly]
Choose one historical figure per month. Research their life and identify: which virtues did they demonstrate? Where did they fail? Which virtue system (Greek, Stoic, Christian, Confucian, warrior) best describes their moral framework? Write a one-page analysis.

### Practice 4: Steel-Man Exercise [Monthly]
Choose the tradition you find least compelling or most foreign. Write a one-page argument FOR that tradition — its strongest case, presented as sympathetically as possible. The ability to steelman what you disagree with is one of the most important intellectual skills in moral reasoning.

### Practice 5: Case Study Discussion [Weekly, With Mentor]
Discuss one moral case per week with your mentor. The case should involve conflicting goods, not just good versus evil: a leader who must choose between loyalty and justice, a person who must choose between honesty and compassion, a warrior who must choose between duty and survival. The discussion should reference at least two virtue traditions.

### Practice 6: Tradition Journal [Ongoing]
Maintain a journal tracking your engagement with each tradition. Record quotes that strike you, questions that arise, connections to other traditions, and honest reactions. This journal is for you — not for evaluation. Its value is in the habit of written reflection on moral questions.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Primary Texts Are Non-Negotiable

The student packet specifies readings from Aristotle, Marcus Aurelius, Confucius, and other primary sources. These are not optional. Summaries, Wikipedia articles, and secondary sources are not substitutes. The entire point of Weathering Storms is that the student encounters these thinkers directly — hears their voice, struggles with their arguments, and forms their own response to the actual text.

This will be difficult. Aristotle is dense. Marcus Aurelius is repetitive. The Analects are aphoristic and require reflection. That difficulty is the point. The student who wrestles with a primary text and extracts meaning from it has done something qualitatively different from the student who reads a summary and nods along.

Your role: ensure the readings happen. Help when the student is stuck — not by explaining the text for them, but by asking questions that help them find the meaning. "What do you think he means by 'the mean'? Can you give an example?" Read the texts yourself if you have not already. You cannot guide discussion of a text you do not know.

### Calibrate Within the Tier

Weathering Storms spans ages 8 through 14. The difference between an 8-year-old and a 14-year-old's capacity for philosophical analysis is vast.

**Ages 8-10:** The student can engage with primary texts in shorter doses with significant mentor support. Read passages aloud together. Discuss as you go. The comparative analysis will be simpler — "Aristotle says virtue is a habit. Confucius says virtue is about relationships. How are those different?" Written responses can be shorter and less formal.

**Ages 11-12:** Independent reading of primary texts becomes feasible for moderately complex passages. Written analyses should be substantive — half a page to a full page. Comparative work begins to show nuance: the student notices not just that traditions differ but why they differ.

**Ages 13-14:** The student should be reading primary texts independently with minimal support. Written analyses should demonstrate genuine argumentation — not just description but evaluation. Steelman exercises become meaningful at this age. Case study discussions can engage real moral complexity.

### Do Not Pick a Winner

The strongest temptation in IC1.2-B — for mentor and student alike — is to rank the traditions. "The Greek system is the best." "Christianity added the most important thing." "The Confucian approach is the most practical." Resist this. The purpose of comparative study is not to crown a champion. It is to understand each tradition deeply enough to learn from it.

This does not mean all traditions are equal in every respect. The student is allowed — encouraged — to evaluate, to express preference, to argue that one tradition handles a particular question better than another. But the evaluation must come after understanding, not before it. The student who dismisses Confucian ethics because it feels unfamiliar has not done the work. The student who engages Confucian ethics seriously, understands its logic, and then argues that it underweights individual moral agency is doing real philosophy.

Your job: ensure understanding precedes evaluation. When the student expresses a preference, ask: "Can you steelman the other side first?" If they cannot, they have not understood it well enough to evaluate it.

### Protect Against Relativism

The opposite danger from picking a winner is concluding that all moral views are equally valid — that virtue is just "whatever each culture happens to believe." This is relativism, and IC1.2 explicitly rejects it. The convergence across traditions is presented as evidence that some moral truths are not culturally relative — that courage, honesty, justice, and the rest are discovered, not invented.

If the student drifts toward "well, everyone has their own truth," push back. "If virtue were just cultural preference, why did civilizations that never met each other arrive at the same core list? If honesty is just one culture's preference, why does every culture we've studied value it? What would it mean for morality if some things really are universal?"

The goal is not dogmatism — it is rigorous pluralism. Many traditions, real differences, genuine convergence on substance.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Tradition Knowledge
**Developing well:** The student can describe at least four traditions accurately: their core virtues, their key thinkers, their distinctive emphases. They can distinguish between traditions — they know what makes Stoicism different from Aristotelian ethics, what Confucius adds that the Greeks lack, what Christianity transforms about the classical picture.

**Developing slowly:** Traditions blur together. The student cannot articulate what makes each tradition distinctive. Knowledge stays at surface level — names and labels without substance.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student actually read the primary texts? (Summaries produce surface knowledge.) Are discussions engaging the specifics of each tradition, or staying at the general level?

### 2. Primary Source Engagement
**Developing well:** The student can quote or closely paraphrase specific passages from primary texts. They reference the texts in discussion and written work. They have genuine reactions — agreement, disagreement, confusion, excitement — that indicate real engagement with the ideas.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot reference specific texts. Their understanding seems derived from summaries or from the student packet itself rather than from the primary sources. Written responses are generic — they could have been written without reading the assigned text.

**Diagnostic questions:** Ask the student to open the text and show you a passage they found interesting or confusing. If they cannot locate one, they have not read carefully enough.

### 3. Comparative Analysis Quality
**Developing well:** Written comparisons demonstrate genuine understanding of both traditions being compared. The student identifies points of agreement and disagreement with specificity. They can explain WHY the traditions differ — connecting the difference to the tradition's larger framework or historical context.

**Developing slowly:** Comparisons are superficial: "The Greeks valued courage and the Chinese valued relationships." No depth, no explanation of why, no engagement with the complexity of either tradition.

### 4. Steel-Manning
**Developing well:** The student can present a tradition they disagree with in its strongest form — charitably, accurately, and persuasively. They can explain what makes the tradition compelling to its adherents. They can identify what it gets right even while maintaining their disagreement.

**Developing slowly:** The student can only argue for what they already believe. Unfamiliar traditions are dismissed or caricatured rather than engaged. "That's weird" or "that doesn't make sense" without effort to understand.

**Diagnostic questions:** "What would Confucius say to your objection? Would he find it convincing? Why or why not?"

### 5. Historical Moral Analysis
**Developing well:** The student can examine a historical figure and identify both virtues and failures with precision. They connect the analysis to specific virtue frameworks: "Alexander failed the Aristotelian virtue of temperance — he could not govern his appetites for power and recognition despite being taught by Aristotle himself."

**Developing slowly:** Moral analysis of historical figures stays at "good person / bad person." No connection to specific virtue frameworks or to the question of why the failure occurred.

### 6. Convergence Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can articulate why cross-cultural convergence matters. They understand the argument: independent arrival at similar conclusions suggests discovery of something real rather than arbitrary invention. They can distinguish this position from both dogmatism and relativism.

**Developing slowly:** The convergence is noted but not analyzed. The student cannot explain what it implies about the nature of moral truth.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Letting the Student Summarize Instead of Analyze

The most common failure mode in comparative study: the student describes each tradition ("The Greeks believed X, the Confucians believed Y") without analyzing the comparison. Description is not analysis. Push past description: "You've told me what each tradition says. Now tell me what the difference means. Why does it matter that Confucius grounds virtue in relationships while Aristotle grounds it in individual character? What follows from that difference?"

### Imposing Your Own Tradition

If you have a strong commitment to a particular tradition — Christian, Stoic, secular humanist, or any other — you will be tempted to steer the student toward it. This is understandable. It is also the wrong move in IC1.2. The student's moral framework should be informed by all these traditions, not pre-loaded with one. If the student asks what you believe, you may tell them — honestly — but make clear that your conclusion is one conclusion among many thoughtful ones, and that their job is to do their own thinking.

### Avoiding the Difficult Parts

Every tradition studied in IC1.2-B has aspects that modern sensibilities find uncomfortable. Greek culture accepted slavery. The Stoics could be read as endorsing passivity. Christianity's demand to love enemies seems impossible. Confucian hierarchy can feel oppressive. Warrior codes included brutal enforcement.

Do not skip these difficulties. Engage them. "Aristotle lived in a slave-holding society. Does that invalidate his ethics? Does it limit them? How?" These questions develop the student's ability to learn from a tradition without endorsing everything it produced — which is the most important intellectual skill for engaging moral philosophy honestly.

### Treating This as a History Lesson

IC1.2-B studies historical traditions, but it is not a history course. The point is not to know when Aristotle lived or to list Confucius's five relationships. The point is to understand each tradition's ARGUMENT about what makes a person good — and to apply that understanding to the student's own moral development. Every discussion should eventually return to: "What does this mean for you? How does this change how you think about virtue?"

### Rushing Through Traditions

The temptation to "cover" all traditions quickly — a week per tradition, check the box — produces shallow understanding. It is better for the student to deeply understand three traditions than to superficially scan five. If you need to spend three weeks on Aristotle because the student is genuinely wrestling with the doctrine of the mean, spend three weeks on Aristotle. The depth matters more than the breadth.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Virtue as Habit (Aristotle)
**Strong:** The student explains Aristotle's argument precisely: virtue is not innate, not a feeling, not a decision — it is a stable disposition formed through repeated practice. They connect this to their own experience in IC1.1 and give concrete examples. They may also note limitations or questions the idea raises.
**Weak:** "Aristotle said virtue is a habit." No elaboration, no personal connection, no evidence of having wrestled with the idea.

### Prompt 2: Greek vs. Confucian
**Strong:** The comparison is specific and substantive. The student identifies Aristotle's emphasis on individual character and practical wisdom versus Confucius's emphasis on relational obligations and ritual propriety. They explain why this difference matters — not just that it exists. They may prefer one approach but can articulate the strength of both.
**Weak:** "The Greeks and Chinese had different virtues." No specificity, no analysis of why the difference matters.

### Prompt 3: Stoic Control
**Strong:** The student explains the Stoic distinction between what is and is not within one's power. They give a personal example: "I cannot control whether my team wins, but I can control how hard I practice and how I respond to losing." The application is genuine, not performative.
**Weak:** Abstract repetition of the Stoic formula without personal application or evidence of understanding.

### Prompt 4: Christian Transformation
**Strong:** The student identifies specifically what the theological virtues add — particularly love as self-sacrifice for others, including enemies. They can articulate how this differs from the Greek emphasis on excellence and the Stoic emphasis on self-governance. They evaluate the addition honestly — whether they find it compelling or not.
**Weak:** "Christians added faith, hope, and love." No explanation of why this matters or how it changes the classical picture.

### Prompt 5: Virtue Failure Analysis
**Strong:** Names a specific figure, identifies specific virtues and specific failures, connects the analysis to a virtue framework, and explains what made the failure possible. "Alexander had Aristotelian courage and tactical prudence but lacked temperance. Power removed the external constraints that had compensated for his lack of self-governance."
**Weak:** "He was a bad person even though he knew about virtue." No specificity, no framework.

### Prompt 6: Convergence
**Strong:** The student can explain the argument from convergence: independent civilizations arriving at similar conclusions suggests discovery rather than invention. They distinguish this from dogmatism (my tradition is the only truth) and from relativism (all traditions are equally right). They articulate what convergence implies about the nature of virtue.
**Weak:** "Different cultures agree about some things." No analysis of what the agreement means.
